primary_segment: commercial jetliners, defense aircraft, space
notes: One of only two makers of large (150+ seat) commercial jetliners; the chain has no third route around it for that segment. Acquired Spirit AeroSystems in a deal completed Dec 8 2025 (see Spirit row).

primary_segment: commercial jetliners, defense aircraft, helicopters, space
notes: The other half of the large-jetliner duopoly with Boeing; incorporated as a Dutch SE, headquartered/operated from Toulouse, France.

primary_segment: regional jets, business jets, defense aircraft
notes: Dominant in regional jets (70-150 seat) but that segment has substitutes (Airbus/Boeing smaller variants, COMAC ARJ21); not a bottleneck for the large-airframe chain.

primary_segment: commercial jetliners (C919, ARJ21), state-backed
notes: China's state jetliner maker; ambition to become a third large-airframe option but as of 2026 still reliant on Western/CFM engines and avionics for the C919 and has not broken the Boeing/Airbus duopoly at scale.

primary_segment: commercial and military jet engines
notes: Member of the jet-engine oligopoly; co-owns CFM International 50/50 with Safran, sole engine supplier for the Boeing 737 and a top option on the Airbus A320. Spun off as the standalone aviation business after GE HealthCare (2023) and GE Vernova (2024) separations.

primary_segment: jet engines (via CFM JV), landing gear, nacelles, seats
notes: Co-owns the CFM International JV 50/50 with GE Aerospace (the CFM56/LEAP line powering most narrowbody jets) -- that JV's chokepoint status is captured under GE Aerospace's row rather than double flagged here. Also a top landing-gear and nacelle supplier, but faces substitutable competition in those lines.

primary_segment: widebody jet engines, defense and marine propulsion
notes: One of only three makers of large widebody jet engines (with GE and Pratt & Whitney); sole or near-sole engine option on several widebody programs (e.g. Trent variants on many 787/A350 aircraft).

primary_segment: jet engines (Pratt & Whitney), avionics and cabin systems (Collins Aerospace), defense systems
notes: Pratt & Whitney is the third member of the widebody/narrowbody engine oligopoly (geared turbofan sole-source on the Airbus A220, an option on the A320neo); Collins Aerospace subsidiary is also a top-tier avionics/systems supplier, compounding RTX's leverage.

primary_segment: jet engine modules and MRO, minority risk-sharing partner
notes: Germany's leading engine maker but participates mainly as a risk-sharing partner (e.g. GEnx, PW1000G, EJ200 programs) rather than as a sole-source prime; substitutable at the partner-share level.

primary_segment: avionics, auxiliary power units, mechanical and electronic systems
notes: Major avionics and systems supplier but competes with Collins Aerospace, Thales and others across most product lines; not a sole-source bottleneck.

primary_segment: avionics, in-flight entertainment, defense electronics, space
notes: Leading European avionics and defense-electronics house; substitutable in most civil avionics lines by Honeywell/Collins, though a near sole option for some European defense electronics programs.

primary_segment: helicopters, defense electronics, aerostructures
notes: Italy's aerospace and defense champion with civil aerostructure and helicopter work plus defense electronics; large but not a chain-wide bottleneck.

primary_segment: defense electronics, combat aircraft, munitions, cyber
notes: UK's largest defense contractor and a major F-35 structures/electronics partner, but the defense-prime layer runs parallel to civil aircraft and has multiple substitutable primes.

primary_segment: aerospace-grade titanium mill products
notes: Historically supplied roughly a third of the world's aerospace titanium (Boeing and Airbus both had direct sourcing/JV ties); Western aerospace has been derisking since 2022 but full substitution takes years given global titanium sponge/ingot capacity limits, keeping this a live chokepoint despite falling Western reliance and partial sanctions on parent Rostec.

primary_segment: specialty metals: titanium, nickel alloys, specialty steel
notes: A qualified Western aerospace-grade titanium melter and the primary hedge/alternative to VSMPO-Avisma; important but framed here as relief valve capacity rather than itself an irreplaceable single point of failure.

exchange: subsidiary of Precision Castparts / Berkshire Hathaway, unlisted
primary_segment: titanium sponge, ingot and mill products
notes: Along with ATI, one of the few qualified Western aerospace titanium producers; owned by Precision Castparts (itself a Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary) since 2012, no separate public financials.

primary_segment: titanium and superalloy structural castings and forgings for airframes and engines
notes: Single-source or near-single-source for many large structural titanium/superalloy castings and forgings used across both Boeing and Airbus programs; taken private by Berkshire Hathaway in 2016. No public financials will be available for the FINANCIALS burst.

primary_segment: engine components, fastening systems, titanium and superalloy castings/forgings
notes: Spun off from Alcoa in 2020; alongside Precision Castparts, one of the very few qualified sources for complex jet-engine and structural castings and aerospace fasteners, with multi-year part qualification lead times that make switching suppliers impractical. Publicly listed, so financials can actually be filled in a later burst (unlike PCC).

primary_segment: fuselage and aerostructures manufacturing
notes: Sole-source builder of the 737 fuselage and major sections of the 767/777/787 for decades -- a genuine physical bottleneck (a single Wichita, KS facility). Acquired by Boeing in a deal completed Dec 8 2025 (~$8.3B incl. debt); per FTC divestiture order, sites/programs tied to Airbus (A220/A320/A350 structures in the US, Morocco, France, UK) were concurrently divested to Airbus. Not separately flagged as a chokepoint because the fuselage bottleneck is now internal to Boeing's own row; kept as its own node since the facility-level single-point-of-failure risk is real and worth tracking independent of ownership.

primary_segment: commercial aircraft leasing
notes: World's largest aircraft lessor by fleet after absorbing GECAS in 2021, but aircraft leasing is a competitive, substitutable financing layer, not a chain bottleneck.

primary_segment: commercial aircraft leasing
notes: Second major listed pure-play lessor for taxonomy balance; substitutable financing layer like AerCap, not a chokepoint.

primary_segment: proprietary aerospace components and aftermarket parts
notes: Business model is built around acquiring many small sole-source, no-substitute proprietary part numbers (pumps, valves, actuators) and pricing accordingly -- each part is a micro-monopoly, but that is a portfolio of narrow chokepoints, not one chain-wide node the whole industry routes through; excluded per the 'do not flag every tier-1 supplier' rule.

primary_segment: FAA-approved replacement parts, aftermarket component repair
notes: Major PMA (parts manufacturer approval) aftermarket supplier that exists specifically to compete with and undercut OEM sole-source pricing; it erodes chokepoints rather than being one.

primary_segment: fighter aircraft (F-35), missiles, space, defense electronics
notes: Sole producer of the F-35, the only fifth-generation fighter in Western multinational production at scale -- a strong candidate chokepoint within the defense-prime branch, but left unflagged here to keep the flagged set focused on nodes that block the core commercial value chain; worth promoting later if the RELATIONSHIPS burst shows allied air forces have no real substitute program.

primary_segment: strategic bombers (B-21), space systems, autonomous systems, defense electronics
notes: Major defense prime and sole B-21 bomber producer, but the bomber program is a narrow niche rather than a chain-wide bottleneck across the broader aerospace value chain.

primary_segment: business jets (Gulfstream), combat vehicles, marine systems, IT services
notes: Diversified defense/aerospace prime (Gulfstream business jets plus land/marine defense); large but faces substitutable competition in each of its lines.
